text: The surgeon begins by carefully dissecting and taking down the omental adhesions attached to the colon and bladder using robotic instruments.
The surgeon carefully dissects and takes down the fistula, focusing on the intricate tissue separation involved in this step of the procedure.
The omentum has been successfully detached from the colon and bladder, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon sweeps the omentum towards the upper abdomen, preparing to proceed with a medial to lateral dissection approach.
The surgeon performs a medial dissection, focusing on separating tissue layers from the central part of the surgical field.
The surgeon performs an upper rectal dissection, carefully manipulating the tissue in the rectal area.
The surgeon manipulates the bowel to increase its mobility, facilitating access and work on the fistula.
The surgeon prepares to take the mesentery, likely using surgical instruments to manipulate and separate it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon ligates the superior hemorrhoidal artery while preserving the left colic artery, indicating a careful approach to managing benign disease during the colectomy.
The surgeon takes the proximal mesentery, utilizing the good view and mobilization achieved earlier in the procedure.
The surgeon begins to take down the fistula, focusing on separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon maneuvers anteriorly to the fistula attachment on the bladder, preparing to dissect this connection.
The surgeon carefully dissects along the colon side to avoid creating a larger hole in the bladder during the fistula takedown.
The surgeon encounters a residual abscess within the bladder wall, visible as a distinct area during the dissection.
The surgeon navigates within the residual abscess, providing a clearer view of the surrounding tissue sides, facilitating smoother dissection.
The surgeon identifies a large lip of the fistula on the colon side, characterized by protruding mucosa, which forms a connection into the bladder wall.
The surgeon continues to dissect and remove tissue, focusing on taking down the fistula between the colon and bladder.
The surgeon observes a significant amount of residual purulence within the abscess cavity, indicating ongoing infection or inflammation in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully dissects along the colon side to avoid creating a larger hole in the bladder, maintaining precision in the surgical field.
The surgeon attempts to visualize the deeper pelvis, maneuvering to gain a clearer view of the anatomical structures in this area.
The uterus is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon attempts to separate the colon from the posterior surface of the uterus, carefully maneuvering to avoid damage to surrounding structures.
The surgeon identifies additional abscess formation within the pelvic cavity, visible as purulent material surrounding the anatomical structures.
The surgeon points out structures located on the outside of the colon wall, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon locates the IR drain adjacent to the colon, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon encounters significant adherence of tissue to the left sidewall during the procedure, indicating dense fibrous attachments that may require careful dissection.
The uterus is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon approaches from the lateral side to safely take down the remaining lateral attachments, ensuring clear visualization of the left ureter during the procedure.
The surgeon navigates directly behind the fundus of the uterus, switching to a lateral approach for improved visualization.
The surgeon attempts to connect the medially dissected plane and proceeds to dissect further into the pelvic region.
The surgeon joins the medially dissected plane, carefully opening up the space further in the pelvic region.

The surgeon works down to the pelvis along the left side wall, carefully dissecting near the residual chronic phlegmon located on the iliac vessels and the left ureter, ensuring visualization of the ureter during the procedure.
The surgeon visualizes the deep pelvis and identifies the remaining attachments as thickened left mesorectum.
The video shows a view of the abscess and fistula area, which appears inflamed and adherent to the bladder and uterus.
The surgeon, having freed up the surrounding tissues, selects the proximal and distal margins for resection.
The surgeon performs a splenic flexure release, addressing the high flexure observed in the patient's colon.
The surgical arms are positioned to facilitate access to the medial aspect of the left kidney, with the surgeon maneuvering towards the pancreas.
The surgeon ensures adequate length of the colon to reach the pelvis and proceeds to divide the colon proximally.
The surgeon uses indocyanine green (ICG) dye to visualize and confirm the location of the optimal margin for resection.
The surgeon completes the division of the tissue using scissors and then applies a surgical stapler to secure the distal end.
The surgeon assesses two healthy edges of the bowel with good blood supply, preparing for an anastomosis beneath the area affected by chronic phlegma, without the need for diversion.
The surgeon moves the resected bowel specimen to the side, clearing the surgical field for further procedures.
The surgeon performs a sewn tension-free anastomosis between the healthy edges of the bowel, ensuring a secure connection without tension.
The surgeon sews the posterior row of the anastomosis, joining the two staple lines of the bowel edges together.
The surgeon performs a proctotomy and a colotomy, creating openings in the rectum and colon respectively.
The surgeon begins inner row suturing, joining the lip of the bowel adjacent to the staple lines together.
The surgeon begins suturing on the left side, carefully placing each suture to align the bowel edges precisely.
The surgeon rotates the bowel to position the canal anteriorly, aligning it for the next step in the anastomosis process.
The surgeon adjusts the position of the bowel before beginning the second suture.
The surgeon begins the inner row suturing of the bowel adjacent to the staple lines, carefully placing each suture in the intended location.
The surgeon begins suturing to the right side, ensuring precise placement of each suture in the surgical field.
The surgeon turns the suture around and channels it over the top, adjusting its position for optimal placement.
The surgeon completes the inner anterior row of sutures, ensuring the tissue is securely approximated.
The surgeon alternates between two sutures, ensuring they meet and are tied together at the midline of the surgical site.
The surgeon brings back the other suture to continue the suturing process, ensuring alignment and closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon carefully imbricates the edges of the bowel, ensuring that the mucosa is not exposed or bunched up between the poles of the suture.
The surgeon cuts one of the sutures and ties the two remaining sutures together at the midline, completing the suture line.
The surgeon takes the original posterior suture that adjoined the staple lines and imbricates it over the top in a Lambert fashion for additional security.
The surgeon sews the newly imbricated suture to the original silk stay suture, ensuring secure attachment and alignment of the bowel edges.
The surgeon prepares to perform a leak test on the newly sutured section of the colon, ensuring the integrity of the anastomosis.
The surgeon pushes the anastomosis into the water, likely as part of a leak test to check for any signs of leakage from the surgical site.
The surgeon performs a leak test by inserting a 19mm proctoscope through the anastomosis to check for any leaks.
The surgeon observes the anastomosis submerged in water, noting the absence of bubbles and good distension, indicating a successful leak test.
The surgeon performs a bladder test by instilling methylene blue mixed with saline into the bladder, observing for good distension.
Residual blood is observed pouring out of the fistula abscess cavity on the dome of the bladder, but there is no visible leakage of methylene blue from the cavity.
The surgeon removes the Foley catheter from the bladder.
The surgeon places Surgicel into the abscess cavity on the dome of the bladder to control oozing blood.
The surgeon places a Blake drain in the pelvis and fills the pelvic cavity with omentum.
